topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery person, a young man with a surprisingly gentle smile, placed the bouquet on the entryway table. Crimson roses, heavy with dew, spilled over the polished wood. I watched him retreat, the sound of his footsteps echoing in the sudden silence. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick intake of air that warmed my chest. I didn't move immediately, just let my gaze drift down to the velvety petals. A slow, deliberate movement pulled my hand toward the nearest bloom, my fingertips brushing the cool, damp surface. The scent, rich and intoxicating, flooded my senses, making my focus narrow entirely to the texture beneath my touch. A low hum started deep in my core, a tightening sensation that demanded attention. I leaned forward, inhaling deeply, letting the heady fragrance wash over me until the edges of my vision softened.

[story 2]

It was just a bunch of lilies, stark white against the dark mahogany of the console. No note, just the flowers left on the doorstep. I picked them up, the stems cool and slick against my palm. A flush began to creep up my neck, spreading across my cheeks like a slow tide. I turned the arrangement over in my hands, appreciating the weight and the delicate structure of the blossoms. Every nerve ending seemed suddenly hyper-sensitive. I walked toward the window, the movement feeling heavy, deliberate. Looking out at the darkening street, I found my gaze snagging on the way the light caught the curve of a petal. A distinct thrumming began beneath my skin, a restless energy that made my muscles feel coiled and ready. I wanted to press the cool petals against my skin, to feel the contrast.

[story 3]

When I opened the door, the vibrant splash of orange and gold hit me first. They were sunflowers, enormous and beaming, utterly unadorned by any accompanying message. I took them in, my fingers wrapping around the thick, sturdy stems. A sudden warmth bloomed low in my abdomen, a distinct shift in my equilibrium. I didn't bother with the table; I carried them directly into the living room, the weight of them a tangible anchor. I set them down on the floor, and the sheer scale of the blooms made me feel small, yet intensely focused. I knelt down, bringing my face close to the thick, golden center, inhaling deeply until my head felt light. A deep, internal stirring began, a building pressure that made my breathing shallow and uneven. I traced the edge of one large leaf with my thumb, a slow, electric current running up my arm.
